label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's look at the question and the text. The question is asking what the author thinks about high school teachers. The text says: "My final issue I would like to call to your attention is teacher's salaries. The high school students of today are America's future. In a way, America's future depends greatly on the high school teachers of America. Saying this, it is sad that teacher's salaries are where they are. While teachers are working harder and harder every day to teach high school students, CEO's of big companies that are driving our countries economy into the ground are getting bonuses. I'm not saying that teacher's salaries should be the same of CEO's of companies, but I do strongly believe that action should be taken to increase the salaries of teachers because they have one of the most important jobs in America - - preparing our country for the future." So the author is saying that teachers' salaries are not high enough ("it is sad that teacher's salaries are where they are") and that they should be increased ("action should be taken to increase the salaries of teachers"). The author also contrasts this with CEOs getting bonuses despite their companies possibly harming the economy. The author clarifies that they don't think teachers' salaries should be the same as CEOs', but they should be increased. The author also says that teachers have one of the most important jobs in America. Looking at the answer choices: (A) the teachers' salaries are not high enough. - This is directly stated in the text. (B) teacher's salaries should be as high as CEO's. - The author explicitly says they don't think this. (C) the teachers work harder than CEO's of companies. - The author says teachers are working harder every day, but not necessarily harder than CEOs. The comparison is about the importance of the job and the disparity in compensation, not who works harder. (D) teaching is more important
